steps.title: Treat the model as ordinary exponential growth
steps.content: Since $f(x)=ab^{x/n}$, let $k=b^{1/n}$. Then the model is $f(x)=ak^x$, where $k$ is the multiplier for each 1-unit increase in $x$. Because $b$ is positive, $k$ is positive. This avoids guessing separate values of $b$ and $n$.

steps.title: Find the 1-unit multiplier
steps.content:
Use the two given values as a ratio:
$$\frac{f(5)}{f(3)}=\frac{112}{7}=16$$
The input increased by $2$, so $k^2=16$. Since $k$ is positive, $k=4$.

steps.title: Move one step from x = 5 to x = 6
steps.content:
From $x=5$ to $x=6$ is one 1-unit increase, so multiply by $k=4$:
$$f(6)=112\cdot 4=448$$
The answer is 448.
